content.problemStatement:
This problem is similar to the problem "MNDIGSUM". The only differences are in the constraints and in the input format. In this problem the range for the base BBB is [2,R][2, R][2,R], the value of nnn can be upto 101210^{12}1012 and the total number of queries is at most 300300300. The differences are described in bold.

Let f(n,B)f(n, B)f(n,B) be the sum of digits of the integer nnn when written in base BBB.

More formally, if n=∑i=0∞aiBin = \sum\limits_{i=0}^{\infty} a_i B^in=i=0∑∞​ai​Bi where aia_iai​ is an integer in the range [0,B−1][0, B-1][0,B−1], then f(n,B)=∑i=0∞aif(n, B) = \sum\limits_{i=0}^{\infty} a_if(n,B)=i=0∑∞​ai​.

Given QQQ queries, each consisting of **two** integers nnn and rrr. Find the value of BBB corresponding to which f(n,B)f(n, B)f(n,B) is minimum for all 2≤B≤r{\bf{2}} \leq B \leq r2≤B≤r. If there are multiple such values, you can print any of them.


Input Format

The first line contains in single integer QQQ, the number of queries
Each of the next QQQ lines contain two space separated integers nnn and rrr respectively.


Output Format

For each query (`n r`) find the value of the base BBB that lies within [2,r][{\bf{2}}, r][2,r] such that f(n,B)f(n, B)f(n,B) is minimum.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer Q, the number of queries. Each of the next Q lines contains two space‑separated integers n and r (2 ≤ n, r ≤ 10^12).
content.outputFormat: For each query output a single integer B (2 ≤ B ≤ r) such that the digit sum f(n,B) is minimum. If several bases give the same minimum, output any of them. Print each answer on its own line.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
To compute f(n,B)f(n, B)f(n,B), first write the integer nnn in the form a0+a1B+a2B2+…a_0 + a_1 B + a_2 B^2 + \dots a0​+a1​B+a2​B2+… such that 0≤ai<B0 \leq a_i < B0≤ai​<B and aia_iai​ is an integer. It can be shown that such a representation is unique. The sum of the digits is then ∑i=0∞ai\sum\limits_{i=0}^{\infty} a_ii=0∑∞​ai​ (to be computed in base 10).
For example to compute f(216,2)f(216, 2)f(216,2), observe that 216=27+26+24+23216 = 2^7 + 2^6 + 2^4 + 2^3216=27+26+24+23, and so the sum of digits is 1+1+1+1=41 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 41+1+1+1=4.
Similarly, to compute  f(216,100)f(216, 100)f(216,100), write 216216216 as 2∗100+162 * 100 + 162∗100+16 and so the sum of digits is 2+16=182 + 16 = 182+16=18.
Test case 111: We have f(216,2)=f(216,3)=4f(216, 2) = f(216, 3) = 4f(216,2)=f(216,3)=4, f(216,4)=6f(216, 4) = 6f(216,4)=6, f(216,5)=8f(216, 5) = 8f(216,5)=8, f(216,6=1)f(216, 6 = 1)f(216,6=1) and finally f(216,7)=12f(216, 7) = 12f(216,7)=12. Clearly the minimum is obtained when B=6B = 6B=6.
Test case 222: Note that f(256,2)=f(256,4)f(256, 2) = f(256, 4)f(256,2)=f(256,4) = 111, therefore both the answers 222 and 444 will be considered correct.
Test case 333: f(31,3)=f(31,5)=3f(31, 3) = f(31, 5) = 3f(31,3)=f(31,5)=3 and f(31,4)=7f(31, 4) = 7f(31,4)=7, therefore both the answers 333 and 555 will be considered correct.

solution.approach:
For a fixed n we need the base B (2≤B≤r) that minimises the sum of digits of n in base B. For B ≤ √n we can evaluate f(n,B) directly by repeatedly dividing n by B – this costs O(log_B n) per base and at most √n bases. For B > √n the representation of n has at most two digits: n = q·B + rem with q = ⌊n/B⌋ < √n and 0 ≤ rem < B. Hence f(n,B) = q + rem = q + (n - q·B). For a given quotient q the digit sum is a linear function of B and is minimal at the largest B that yields this q, i.e. B = ⌊n/q⌋. Therefore we enumerate all possible q from 1 to √n, compute B = n//q and, if B lies in [2,r], evaluate the digit sum using the formula above. We also evaluate the extreme base B = r (if r > √n) because it may not be produced by the q‑enumeration. The overall per‑query work is O(√n) which is ≤ 10^6 for the maximal n, easily fast for Q ≤ 300. The algorithm keeps the best base found and its digit sum.

Edge cases handled:
- r < √n – only the direct brute over [2,r] is performed.
- r = n – base n gives digit sum 1, which is optimal.
- Multiple optimal bases – any one is kept.
- Small n (e.g., n = 2) – loops work correctly.
solution.timeComplexity: O(Q·√n_max) ≤ 300·10^6 operations in the worst case, well within limits.
